title.en: Qwen3-VL-2B: Automated Image Captioning & Accessibility Alt-Text Pipeline

description.en: Use Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct to generate rich alt-text captions for images locally, build a multi-tier captioning pipeline (brief, standard, detailed), and create a Homie accessibility plugin that makes visual content inclusive for screen readers.

story_variants.en: ## Making Images Speak: Accessibility Captioning with Qwen3-VL-2B Vaathiyaar here! Over one billion people worldwide live with some form of visual impairment, and the web is full of images with empty `alt` attributes. What if you could run a local AI that automatically generates high-quality alt-text for every image in your project — no cloud API, no per-image cost, no privacy concerns? That's what we'll build with **Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct**. This lesson goes beyond basic captioning. You'll create a multi-tier pipeline that produces brief, standard, and detailed descriptions, each tuned for a different accessibility context. ### Why Multi-Tier Captions? | Tier | Length | Use Case | Example | |---|---|---|---| | Brief | 5-15 words | `alt` attribute in `` tags | "A golden retriever playing in a park" | | Standard | 25-50 words | Screen reader descriptions | "A golden retriever catches a red frisbee mid-air in a sunny park, with trees in the background" | | Detailed | 75-150 words | Long description for complex images | Full scene description with spatial layout, colors, mood | Different contexts demand different verbosity. A thumbnail needs a brief caption; an infographic needs a detailed one. ### Loading the Model ```python from transformers import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration, AutoProcessor import torch model_name = "Qwen/Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct" processor = AutoProcessor.from_pretrained(model_name) model = Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration.from_pretrained( model_name, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto" ) ``` At 2B parameters with float16, this fits comfortably in ~3GB VRAM — even a modest GPU handles it. ### The Multi-Tier Captioning Function ```python TIER_PROMPTS = { "brief": "Describe this image in one short sentence (under 15 words). " "Be factual and concise. No opinions or interpretations.", "standard": "Write an accessibility alt-text description of this image " "in 25-50 words. Include key subjects, actions, colors, " "and spatial relationships.", "detailed": "Write a comprehensive image description in 75-150 words " "for someone who cannot see it. Cover subjects, actions, " "background, lighting, mood, colors, and spatial layout." } def caption_image(image, tier="standard"): prompt = TIER_PROMPTS.get(tier, TIER_PROMPTS["standard"]) messages = [ {"role": "user", "content": [ {"type": "image", "image": image}, {"type": "text", "text": prompt} ]} ] text = processor.apply_chat_template( messages, tokenize=False, add_generation_prompt=True ) inputs = processor( text=[text], images=[image], return_tensors="pt" ).to(model.device) output = model.generate( **inputs, max_new_tokens=200, temperature=0.3 ) return processor.decode( output[0][inputs["input_ids"].shape[-1]:], skip_special_tokens=True ).strip() ``` A moderate temperature of 0.3 keeps captions accurate while allowing natural phrasing. ### Batch Captioning a Directory ```python from pathlib import Path from PIL import Image def batch_caption(image_dir, tier="standard"): results = {} for img_path in sorted(Path(image_dir).glob("*")): if img_path.suffix.lower() in {".jpg", ".jpeg", ".png", ".webp"}: image = Image.open(img_path).convert("RGB") results[img_path.name] = { "caption": caption_image(image, tier), "tier": tier } return results ``` The suffix filter ensures we only process actual image files — directories with mixed content won't crash the pipeline. ### Building the Homie Accessibility Plugin ```python class AccessibilityCaptionPlugin: def __init__(self, model, processor): self.model = model self.processor = processor def alt_text(self, image_path, tier="standard"): image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB") return caption_image(image, tier) def all_tiers(self, image_path): image = Image.open(image_path).convert("RGB") return { t: caption_image(image, t) for t in TIER_PROMPTS } def html_tag(self, image_path, tier="brief"): alt = self.alt_text(image_path, tier) fname = Path(image_path).name return f'' ``` Homie calls `plugin.alt_text(path)` for a single caption or `plugin.all_tiers(path)` to get brief, standard, and detailed at once — then picks the right one for the context. > **Key Insight:** Accessibility isn't an afterthought — it's a pipeline stage. By running Qwen3-VL-2B locally, you can generate multi-tier alt-text for every image in your project at build time, with zero cloud cost and full privacy. The result: inclusive content that works for screen readers, search engines, and low-bandwidth users alike.

animation_sequence.content: Billions of images on the web have empty alt attributes. Qwen3-VL-2B-Instruct can fix that — generating multi-tier accessibility captions locally with just 3GB of VRAM. Brief for thumbnails, standard for screen readers, detailed for complex visuals. Let's build an automated captioning pipeline.

steps.explanation: Import Qwen3VLForConditionalGeneration — the vision-language model class that accepts both image and text inputs for multimodal understanding.
Import torch for dtype control. Using float16 halves memory from ~6GB to ~3GB — critical for running on consumer GPUs.
The 2B-Instruct variant is instruction-tuned, making it responsive to specific captioning prompts like length and style constraints.
Load with float16 precision and automatic device mapping. The model figures out GPU vs CPU placement for each layer.
Define three prompt tiers. Each tier constrains the model to a different verbosity level — the same model, tuned by prompt alone.
caption_image takes a PIL Image and tier name. It falls back to 'standard' if an unknown tier is requested.
Build multimodal messages in the OpenAI-style format that Qwen3-VL expects — mixing image and text content blocks.
apply_chat_template converts the message list into the token format the model was trained on, inserting image placeholder tokens.
Generate with temperature 0.3 — low enough for factual accuracy, high enough for natural sentence flow in captions.
Decode only the newly generated tokens (skip the input) and strip whitespace. This gives a clean caption string ready for alt attributes.
batch_caption scans a directory and generates captions for every image file it finds, returning a filename-to-caption mapping.
Filter by known image extensions to avoid processing non-image files. The suffix check is case-insensitive for robustness.
Convert to RGB handles RGBA (PNG transparency) and grayscale images — the model expects 3-channel input.
AccessibilityCaptionPlugin wraps the captioning function into a clean interface Homie can call without knowing model internals.
alt_text() is the primary method — give it an image path and tier, get back a ready-to-use alt-text string.
all_tiers() generates brief, standard, and detailed captions in one call — useful when you need all three for different contexts.
html_tag() goes one step further: it returns a complete tag with the alt attribute already populated. Drop it straight into HTML.

items.right: Screen readers describe the full scene

items.left: Requires cloud API and per-call cost
items.right: Runs locally with zero cloud dependency

animation_sequence.message: You've built an accessibility captioning pipeline! Qwen3-VL-2B now generates multi-tier alt-text locally — making visual content inclusive for screen readers, searchable for SEO, and private by design.

instruction.en: Build a `CaptionTierManager` class that manages multi-tier image captions. In `__init__`, accept a `tiers` dict mapping tier names to max word counts (default: `{"brief": 15, "standard": 50, "detailed": 150}`). Implement `generate_mock_caption(filename, tier)` that returns a mock caption string: `"<tier> caption for <filename>"` (e.g., `"brief caption for cat.jpg"`). If the tier is unknown, raise a `ValueError` with message `"Unknown tier: <tier>"`. Implement `caption_all_tiers(filename)` returning a dict mapping each tier name to its mock caption. Implement `get_tier_names()` returning a sorted list of tier names. Implement `word_limit(tier)` returning the max word count for that tier (raise `ValueError` for unknown tiers).

practice_challenges.starter_code:
class CaptionTierManager:
    def __init__(self, tiers=None):
        # Default tiers: brief=15, standard=50, detailed=150
        pass

    def generate_mock_caption(self, filename: str, tier: str) -> str:
        # Return "<tier> caption for <filename>"
        # Raise ValueError for unknown tier
        pass

    def caption_all_tiers(self, filename: str) -> dict:
        # Return {tier_name: caption} for all tiers
        pass

    def get_tier_names(self) -> list:
        # Return sorted list of tier names
        pass

    def word_limit(self, tier: str) -> int:
        # Return max word count for the tier
        # Raise ValueError for unknown tier
        pass

# Test it:
mgr = CaptionTierManager()
print(mgr.generate_mock_caption("dog.jpg", "brief"))
print(mgr.word_limit("brief"))
print(mgr.get_tier_names())

all_caps = mgr.caption_all_tiers("sunset.png")
print(len(all_caps))
print(all_caps["standard"])

try:
    mgr.generate_mock_caption("x.jpg", "ultra")
except ValueError as e:
    print(e)
practice_challenges.expected_output:
brief caption for dog.jpg
15
['brief', 'detailed', 'standard']
3
standard caption for sunset.png
Unknown tier: ultra

en: Store tiers as self.tiers = tiers or {"brief": 15, "standard": 50, "detailed": 150}
In generate_mock_caption, check `if tier not in self.tiers: raise ValueError(f"Unknown tier: {tier}")` before building the string
caption_all_tiers is simply: return {t: self.generate_mock_caption(filename, t) for t in self.tiers}

practice_challenges.test_code:
mgr = CaptionTierManager()
assert mgr.generate_mock_caption("cat.jpg", "brief") == "brief caption for cat.jpg"
assert mgr.generate_mock_caption("img.png", "detailed") == "detailed caption for img.png"
assert mgr.word_limit("brief") == 15
assert mgr.word_limit("standard") == 50
assert mgr.word_limit("detailed") == 150
assert mgr.get_tier_names() == ["brief", "detailed", "standard"]
all_c = mgr.caption_all_tiers("photo.jpg")
assert len(all_c) == 3
assert all_c["brief"] == "brief caption for photo.jpg"
assert all_c["standard"] == "standard caption for photo.jpg"
try:
    mgr.generate_mock_caption("x.jpg", "ultra")
    assert False, "Should have raised ValueError"
except ValueError as e:
    assert "Unknown tier: ultra" in str(e)
try:
    mgr.word_limit("mega")
    assert False, "Should have raised ValueError"
except ValueError as e:
    assert "mega" in str(e)
custom = CaptionTierManager(tiers={"short": 10, "long": 200})
assert custom.get_tier_names() == ["long", "short"]
assert custom.word_limit("short") == 10

instruction.en: Build an `AltTextHTMLGenerator` class that generates HTML `` tags with alt-text. In `__init__`, accept an optional `default_tier` string (default: `"standard"`). Implement `make_alt_text(filename, tier)` that returns `" description of "` (e.g., `"standard description of photo.jpg"`). Implement `img_tag(src, filename=None, tier=None)` that generates ``. If `filename` is None, extract it from `src` (the part after the last `/`). If `tier` is None, use `default_tier`. Implement `batch_tags(src_list)` that returns a list of img tags using default_tier for all images.

practice_challenges.starter_code: class AltTextHTMLGenerator: def __init__(self, default_tier="standard"): pass def make_alt_text(self, filename: str, tier: str) -> str: # Return " description of " pass def img_tag(self, src: str, filename=None, tier=None) -> str: # Build pass def batch_tags(self, src_list: list) -> list: # Return list of img tags for each src pass # Test it: gen = AltTextHTMLGenerator() print(gen.make_alt_text("cat.jpg", "brief")) print(gen.img_tag("images/dog.png")) print(gen.img_tag("pic.jpg", tier="detailed")) tags = gen.batch_tags(["a/one.jpg", "b/two.png"]) print(len(tags)) print(tags[0])
practice_challenges.expected_output: brief description of cat.jpg 2

en: Extract filename from src with src.split('/')[-1] when filename is None
Use tier = tier or self.default_tier to fall back to the default
batch_tags is: return [self.img_tag(src) for src in src_list]

practice_challenges.test_code: gen = AltTextHTMLGenerator() assert gen.make_alt_text("x.jpg", "brief") == "brief description of x.jpg" assert gen.make_alt_text("y.png", "detailed") == "detailed description of y.png" t1 = gen.img_tag("images/cat.jpg") assert t1 == '' t2 = gen.img_tag("dog.png", tier="brief") assert t2 == '' t3 = gen.img_tag("a/b/c.jpg", filename="custom.jpg") assert 'alt="standard description of custom.jpg"' in t3 batch = gen.batch_tags(["a/1.jpg", "b/2.png", "c/3.webp"]) assert len(batch) == 3 assert 'alt="standard description of 1.jpg"' in batch[0] assert 'alt="standard description of 3.webp"' in batch[2] gen2 = AltTextHTMLGenerator(default_tier="brief") t4 = gen2.img_tag("photo.jpg") assert 'alt="brief description of photo.jpg"' in t4
